terms.definition: A ballot submitted by a voter who is unable to visit their polling place on Election Day.
A state where both major parties have a roughly equal chance of winning, making them the focus of campaign efforts.
A meeting of party members where participants openly discuss and vote on candidates.
A primary election where only voters registered with a specific party can participate.
A person authorized to represent others at a political convention, pledged to a specific candidate.
Races for offices listed below the presidential race on the ballot.
The process of voting before Election Day during a designated period.
The body of 538 electors who formally elect the president. 270 votes needed to win.
An elector who votes against their pledge in the Electoral College.
Federal Election Commission — the agency that regulates campaign finance.
A Senate procedure where debate is extended to delay or block a vote on a bill.
Drawing electoral district boundaries to give unfair advantage to a party.
A current office holder who is running for re-election.
A ballot mailed to voters, allowing them to vote from home.
Federal elections held halfway through a president's four-year term.
A primary where any registered voter can participate regardless of party.
Political Action Committee — pools contributions to donate to campaigns.
A party's formal statement of principles and policy positions.
The total number of votes cast by citizens across the country.
The smallest geographic unit for organizing elections.
A state-level election where voters choose which candidate will represent a party.
A ballot cast when voter eligibility is in question, counted after verification.
A system where voters rank candidates by preference.
Redrawing electoral district boundaries after each census.
A direct vote by citizens on a specific policy proposal or law.
A second election when no candidate achieves the required threshold.
An independent committee that can raise unlimited funds but cannot coordinate with campaigns.
The day when the largest number of states hold primaries simultaneously.
A Democratic Party delegate not bound by primary results.
A state that could be won by either major party in a given election.
Any political party other than the Democratic and Republican parties.
The percentage of eligible voters who cast ballots in an election.
A candidate whose name voters must write on the ballot.
